My another concern is only deep learning methods are presented as baselines. It would be great to compare with standard NLP techniques such as Bag of Words followed by SVM. I am sure these would be outperformed by neural networks, but the numbers would give a sense of how easy/hard the current problem setup is. 

Did the authors look at the success and failure cases of the algorithm? Are there any insights that can be drawn from such analysis that can inform design of future models? 

Overall I think the research direction of using ML for theorem proving is an interesting one. However, I also feel the paper is quite opaque. Many parts of how the data is constructed is unclear (atleast to someone with little knowledge in ITPs). If authors can revise the text to make it clearer – it would be great. The baseline models seem to perform quite well, however there are no insights into what kind of ability the models are lacking. Authors mention that they are unable to perform logical reasoning – but that’s a very vague statement. Some examples of mistakes might help make the message clearer. Further, since I am not well versed with the ITP literature it’s not possible for me to judge how valuable is this dataset. From the references, it seems like it’s drawn from a set of benchmark conjectures/proofs used in the ITP community – so its possibly a good dataset. 

My current rating is a weak reject, but if the authors address my concerns I would change to an accept.

The authors present a dataset extraction method, dataset and first interesting results for machine-learning supported higher order logic theorem proving. The experimental results are impressively good for a first baseline and with an accuracy higher than 0.83 in relevance classification a lot better than chance, and encourage future research in this direction. The paper is well-written in terms of presentation and argumentation and leaves little room for criticism. The related work seems to be well-covered, though I have to note that I am not an expert for automated theorem proving.

The paper contains an interesting idea, and after the revision of 3rd Jan, the presentation is clear enough as well. (Although I find it now contains an odd repetition where related work is presented first in section 2, and then later in section 3.2).

The paper explores a simple approach to learning reward functions for reinforcement learning from visual observations of expert trajectories for cases were only little training data is available. To obtain descriptive rewards even under such challenging conditions the method re-uses a pre-trained neural network as feature extractor (this is similar to a large body of work on task transfer with neural nets in the area of computer vision) and represents the reward function as a weighted distance to features for automatically extracted "key-frames" of the provided expert trajectories.

The paper is well written and explains all involved concepts clearly while also embedding the presented approach in the literature on inverse reinforcement learning (IRL). The resulting algorithm is appealing due to its simplicity and could prove useful for many real world robotic applications. I have three main issues with the paper in its current form, if these can be addressed I believe the paper would be significantly strengthened:
1) Although the recursive splitting approach for extracting the "key-frames" seems reasonable and the feature selection is well motivated I am missing two baselines in the experiments:
   - what happens if the feature selection is disabled and the distance between all features is used ? will this immediately break the procedure ? If not, what is the trade-off here ? 
   - an even simpler baseline than what is proposed in the paper would be the following procedure: simply use all frames of the recorded trajectories, calculate the distance to them in feature space and weights them according to their time as in the approach proposed in the paper. How well would that work ?
2) I understand the desire to combine the extracted reward function with a simple RL method but believe the used simple controller could potentially introduce a significant bias in the experiments since it requires initialization from an expert trajectory. As a direct consequence of this initialization the RL procedure is already started close to a good solution and the extracted reward function is potentially only queried in a small region around what was observed in the initial set of images (perhaps with the exception of the human demonstrations). Without an additional experiment it is thus unclear how well the presented approach will work in combination with other RL methods for training the controller.
3) I understand that the low number of available images excludes training a deep neural net directly for the task at hand but one has to wonder how other baselines would do. What happens if one uses a random projection of the images to form a feature vector? How well would a distance measure using raw images (e.g. L2 norm of image differences) or a distance measure based on the first principal components work? It seems that occlusions etc. would exclude them from working well but without empirical evidence it is hard to confirm this.

Minor issues:
- Page 1: "make use of ideas about imitation" reads a bit awkwardly
- Page 3: "We use the Inception network pre-trained ImageNet" -> pre-trained for ImageNet classification
- Page 4: the definition of the transition function for the stochastic case seems broken
- Page 6: "efficient enough to evaluate" a bit strangely written sentence

Additional comments rather than real issues:
- The paper is mainly of empirical nature, little actual learning is performed to obtain the reward function and no theoretical advances are needed. This is not necessarily bad but makes the empirical evaluation all the more important. 
- While I liked the clear exposition the approach -- in the end -- boils down to computing quadratic distances to features of pre-extracted "key-frames", it is nice that you make a connection to standard IRL approaches in Section 2.1 but one could argue that this derivation is not strictly necessary.

The reviewers (and I) welcome new approaches to evaluation, and this work appears to be a sound contribution to that space. I suggest that, because the paper's incremental findings are of somewhat narrower interest, it is a good fit for a workshop, so that the ideas can be discussed at ICLR and further developed into a more mature publication.

First, let me praise the authors for generating and releasing an NLP data set: a socially useful task.

The authors use an algorithm to generate a 500-cluster-per-language data set in semantic similarity. This brings up a few points.

1. If the point of using the algorithm is to be scalable, why release such a small data set? It's roughly the same order of magnitude as the data sets released in the SemEval tasks over the recent years. I would have expected something orders of magnitude larger.

2. The authors hand checked a small subset of the clusters: they found one where it was ambiguous, and should probably have been removed. Mechanical Turk can scale pretty well -- why not post-facto filter all of the clusters using MT? This is (in effect) how ImageNet was created, and it has millions of items.

3. Evaluating data set papers is an tricky issue. What makes a data set "good" or publishable? There are a number of medium-sized NLP data sets released every year (e.g., through SemEval). Those are designed to address tasks in NLP that people find interesting. I don't know of a data set that exactly addresses the task that the authors propose: the task is trying to address the idea of semantic similarity, which has had multiple data sets thrown at it since SemEval 2012. I wish that the paper had included comparisons to show that the particular task / data combination is better suited for analyzing semantic similarity than other existing data sets.

Two final notes:

A. This paper doesn't seem very well-suited to ICLR.  New NLP data sets may be indirectly useful for evaluating word embeddings (and hence representations). But, I didn't learn much from the paper: GloVe is empirically less good for semantic similarity than other embeddings? If true, why? That would be interesting.

B. The first proposal for the "put a word into a cluster and see if it stands out" task (in the context of human evaluation of topic models), is

Jonathan Chang, Jordan Boyd-Graber, Chong Wang, Sean Gerrish, and David M. Blei. Reading Tea Leaves: HowHumans Interpret Topic Models. Neural Information Processing Systems, 2009

which deserves a citation, I think.

This paper presents an algorithm for approximating the solution of certain time-evolution PDEs. The paper presents an interesting learning-based approach to solve such PDEs. The idea is to alternate between:
1. sampling points in space-time
2. generating solution to PDE at "those" sampled points
3. regressing a space-time function to satisfy the latter solutions at the sampled points (and hopefully generalize beyond those points).

I actually find the proposed algorithm interesting, and potentially useful in practice. The classic grid-based simulation of PDEs is often too expensive to be practical, due to the curse of dimensionality. Hence, learning the solution of PDEs makes a lot of sense for practical settings. On the other hand, as the authors point out, simply running gradient descent on the regression loss function does not work, because of the non-differentiablity of the "min" that shows up in the studied PDEs.

Therefore, I think the proposed idea is actually very interesting approach to learning the PDE solution in presence of non-differentability, which is indeed a "challenging" setup for numerically solving PDEs.

The paper motivates the problem (time-evolution PDE with "min" operator applied to the spatial derivatives) by applications in control thery, but I think there is more direct interest in such problems for the machine learning community, and even deep learning community. For example

This paper proposes an extension of PixelCNN method that can be conditioned on text and spatially-structured constraints (segmentation / keypoints). It is similar to Reed 2016a except the extension is built on top of PixelCNN instead of GAN. After reading the author's comment, I realized the argument is not that conditional PixelCNN is much better than conditional GAN. I think the authors can add more discussions about pros and cons of each model in the paper. I agree with the other reviewer that some analysis of training and generation time would be helpful. I understand it takes O(N) instead of O(1) for PixelCNN method to do sampling, but is that the main reason that the experiments can only be conducted in low resolution (32 x 32)? I also think since there are not quantitative comparisons, it makes more sense to show more visualizations than 3 examples. Overall, the generated results look reasonably good and have enough diversity. The color mistake is an issue where the author has provided some explanations in the comment. I would say the technical novelty is incremental since the extension is straightforward and similar to previous work. I lean toward accepting because it is very relevant to ICLR community and it provides a good opportunity for future investigation and comparison between different deep image synthesis methods.

While I understand the difficulty of collecting audio data from animals, I think this type of feature engineering does not go in the right direction. I would rather see a model than learns the feature representation from data.  I would think it should be possible to collect a more substantial corpus in zoos / nature etc, and then train a generative model. The underlying learned feature representation could be then used to feed a classifier. I'm not familiar with the particularities of this task, it's hard to judge the improvements by using chirplets.

Pros:
* Clearly written.
* New model mLSTM which seems to be useful according to the results.
* Some interesting experiments on big data.

Cons:
* Number of parameters in comparisons of different models is missing.
* mLSTM is behind some other models in most tasks.

This paper proposes an extension of the multiplicative RNN [1] where the authors apply the same reparametrization trick to the weight matrices of the LSTM. 

The paper proposes some interesting tricks, but none of them seems to be very crucial. For instance, in Eq. (16), the authors propose to multiply the output gate inside the activation function in order to alleviate the saturation problem in logistic sigmoid or hyperbolic tangent. Also, the authors share m_t across the inference of different gating units and cell-state candidates, at the end this brings only 1.25 times increase on the number of model parameters. Lastly, the authors use a variant of RMSProp where they add an additional hyper-parameter $\ell$ and schedule it across the training time. It would be nicer to apply the same tricks to other baseline models and show the improvement with regard to each trick.

With the new architectural modification to the LSTM and all the tricks combined, the performance is not as great as we would expect. Why didn’t the authors apply batch normalization, layer normalization or zoneout to their models? Was there any issue with applying one of those regularization or optimization techniques? 

At the fourth paragraph of Section 4.4, where the authors connect dynamic evaluation with fast weights is misleading. I find it a bit hard to connect dynamic evaluation as a variant of fast weights. Fast weights do not use test error signal. In the paper, the authors claim that “dynamic evaluation uses the error signal and gradients to update the weights, which potentially increases its effectiveness, but also limits its scope to conditional generative modelling, when the outputs can be observed after they are predicted”, and I am afraid to tell that this assumption is very misleading. We should never assume that test label information is given at the inference time. The test label information is there to evaluate the generalization performance of the model. In some applications, we may get the label information at test time, e.g., stock prediction, weather forecasting, however, in many other applications, we don’t. For instance, in machine translation, we don't know what's the best translation at the end, unlike weather forecasting. Also, it would be fair to apply dynamic evaluation to all the other baseline models as well to compare with the BPC score 1.19 achieved by the proposed mLSTM.

The quality of the work is not that bad, but the novelty of the paper is not that good either. The performance of the proposed model is oftentime worse than other methods, and it is only better when dynamic evaluation is coupled together. However, dynamic evaluation can improve the other methods as well.

[1] Ilya et al., “Generating Text with Recurrent Neural Networks”, ICML’11

This paper presents a model for video captioning with both soft and hard attention, using a C3D network for the encoder and a RNN for the decoder. Experiments are presented on YouTube2Text, M-VAD, and MSR-VTT. While the ideas of image captioning with soft and hard attention, and video captioning with soft attention, have already been demonstrated in previous work, the main contribution here is the specific architecture and attention over different layers of the CNN.

The work is well presented and the experiments clearly show the benefit of attention over multiple layers. However, in light of previous work in captioning, the contribution and resulting insights is too incremental for a conference paper at ICLR. Further experiments and analysis of the main contribution would strengthen the paper, but I would recommend resubmission to a more suitable venue.

The authors apply the image captioning architecture of Xu et al. 2015 to video captioning. The model is extended to have attention over multiple layers of the ConvNet instead of just a single layer. Experiments on YouTube2Text, M-VAD and MSR-VTT show that this works better than only using one of the layers at a time.

I think this is solid work on the level of a well-executed course project or a workshop paper. The model makes sense, it is adequately described, and the experiments show that attending over multiple layers works better than attending over any one layer in isolation. Unfortunately, I don't think there is enough to get excited about here from a technical perspective and it's not clear what value the paper brings to the community. Other aspects of the paper, such as including the hard attention component, don't seem to add to the paper but take up space. 

If the authors want to contribute a detailed, focused exploration of multi-level features this could become a more valuable paper, but in that case I would expect a much more thorough exploration of the choices and tradeoffs of different schemes without too many spurious aspects such as video features, hard attention, etc.

Thank you for an interesting read. The ideas presented have a good basis of being true, but the experiments are rather too simple. It would be interesting to see more empirical evidence.

Pros
- The approach seems to decrease the training time, which is of prime importance in deep learning. Although, that comes at a price of slightly more complex model.
- There is a grounded theory for sum-product functions which is basis for the compositional architecture described in the paper. Theoretically, any semiring and kernel could be used for the model which decreases need for handcrafting the structure of the model, which is a big problem in existing convolutional neural networks.

Cons
- The experiments are on very simple dataset NORB. Although, it is great to understand a model's dynamics on a simpler dataset, some analysis on complex datasets are important to act as empirical evidence. The compositional kernel approach is compared to convolutional neural networks, hence it is only fair to compare said results on large datasets such as Imagenet.

Minor
- Section 3.4 claims that CKMs model symmetries of objects. It felt that ample justification was not provided for this claim

This paper proposes a new learning framework called "compositional kernel machines" (CKM). It combines two ideas: kernel methods and sum-product network (SPN). CKM first defines leaf kernels on elements of the query and training examples, then it defines kernel recursively (similar to sum-product network). This paper has shown that the evaluation CKM can be done efficiently using the same tricks in SPN.

Positive: I think the idea in this paper is interesting. Instance-based learning methods (such as SVM with kernels) have been successful in the past, but have been replaced by deep learning methods (e.g. convnet) in the past few years. This paper investigate an unexplored area of how to combine the ideas from kernel methods and deep networks (SPN in this case). 

Negative: Although the idea of this paper is interesting, this paper is clearly very preliminary. In its current form, I simply do not see any advantage of the proposed framework over convnet. I will elaborate below.

1) One of the most important claims of this paper is that CKM is faster to learn than convnet. I am not clear why that is the case. Both CKM and convnet use gradient descent during learning, why would CKM be faster?

Also during inference, the running time of convnet only depends on its network structure. But for CKM, in addition to the network structure, it also depends on the size of training set. From this perspective, it does not seem CKM is very scalable when the training size is big. That is probably why this paper has to use all kinds of specialized data structures and tricks (even on a fairly simple dataset like NORB)

2) I am having a hard time understanding what the leaf kernel is capturing. For example, if the "elements" correspond to raw pixel intensities, a leaf kernel essentially compares the intensity value of a pixel in the query image with that in a training image. But in this case, wouldn't you end up comparing a lot of background pixels across these two images (which does not help with recognition)?

I think it probably helps to explain Sec 3.1 a bit better. In its current form, this part is very dense and hard to understand.

3) It is also not entirely clear to me how you would design the architecture of the sum-product function. The example is Sec 3.1 seems to be fairly arbitrary.

4) The experiment section is probably the weakest part. NORB is a very small and toy-ish dataset by today's standard. Even on this small dataset, the proposed method is only slighly better than SVM (it is not clear whether "SVM" in Table 2 is linear SVM or kernel SVM. If it is linear SVM, I suspect the performance of "SVM" will be even higher when you use kernel SVM), and far worse than convnet. The proposed method only shows improvement over convnet on synthetic datasets (NORB compositions, NORM symmetries)

Overall, I think this paper has some interesting ideas. But in its current form, it is a bit too preliminary and more work is needed to show its advantage. Having said that, I acknowledge that in the machine learning history, many important ideas seem pre-mature when they were first proposed, and it took time for these ideas to develop.

The work introduced a new form of regularization for denoising autoencoders, which explicitly enforces robustness in the encoding phrase w.r.t. input perturbation. The author motivates the regularization term as minimizing the conditional entropy of the encoding given the input. The modifier denoising autoencoders is evaluated on some synthetic datasets as well as MNIST, along with regular auto-encoders and denoising autoencoders. The work is fairly similar to several existing extensions to auto-encoders, e.g., contractive auto encoders, which the author did not include in the comparison.   The experiment section needs more polishing. More details should be provided to help understand the figures in the section.

This is a nice proposal, and could lead to more efficient training of
recurrent nets. I would really love to see a bit more experimental evidence.
I asked a few questions already but didn't get any answer so far.
Here are a few other questions/concerns I have:

- Is the resulting model still a universal approximator? (providing large enough hidden dimensions and number of layers)
- More generally, can one compare the expressiveness of the model with the equivalent model without the orthogonal matrices? with the same number of parameters for instance?
- The experiments are a bit disappointing as the number of distinct input/output
sequences were in fact very small and as noted by the authr, training
becomes unstable (I didn't understand what "success" meant in this case).
The authors point that the experiment section need to be expanded, but as
far as I can tell they still haven't unfortunately.

The program committee appreciates the authors' response to concerns raised in the reviews. Unfortunately, reviews are not leaning sufficiently towards acceptance. Reviewers have concerns about the relationships of this work to existing work in literature (both in terms of a discussion to clarify the novelty, and in terms of more complete empirical comparisons). Authors are strongly encouraged to incorporate reviewer feedback in future iterations of the work.

The paper is an extension of the matching networks by Vinyals et al. in NIPS2016. Instead of using all the examples in the support set during test, the method represents each class by the mean of its learned embeddings. The training procedure and experimental setting are very similar to the original matching networks. I am not completely sure about its advantages over the original matching networks. It seems to me when dealing with 1-shot case, these two methods are identical since there is only one example seen in this class, so the mean of the embedding is the embedding itself. When dealing with 5-shot case, original matching networks compute the weighted average of all examples, but it is at most 5x cost. The experimental results reported for prototypical nets are only slightly better than matching networks. I  think it is a simple, straightforward,  novel extension, but I am not fully convinced its advantages.

The paper introduces some interesting architectural ideas for character-aware sequence modelling. However, as pointed out by reviewers and from my own reading of the paper, this paper fails badly on the evaluation front. First, some of the evaluation tasks are poorly defined (e.g. question task). Second, the tasks look fairly simple, whereas there are "standard" tasks such as language modelling datasets (one of the reviewers suggests TREC, but other datasets such as NANT, PTB, or even the Billion Word Corpus) which could be used here. Finally, the benchmarks presented against are weak. There are several character-aware language models which obtain robust results on LM data which could readily be adapted to sentence representation learning, eg. Ling et al. 2016, or Chung et al. 2016, which should have been compared against. The authors should look at the evaluations in these papers and consider them for a future version of this paper. As it stands, I cannot recommend acceptance in its current form.

This paper extends boosting to the task of learning generative models of data. The strong learner is obtained as a geometric average of “weak learners”, which can themselves be normalized (e.g. VAE) or un-normalized (e.g. RBMs) generative models (genBGM), or a classifier trained to discriminate between the strong learner at iteration T-1 and the true data distribution (discBGM). This latter method is closely related to Noise Contrastive Estimation, GANs, etc.

The approach benefits from strong theoretical guarantees, with strict conditions under which each boosting iteration is guaranteed to improve the log-likelihood. The downside of the method appears to be the lack of normalization constant for the resulting strong learner and the use of heuristics to weight each weak learner (which seems to matter in practice, from Sec. 3.2). The discriminative approach further suffers from an expensive training procedure: each round of boosting first requires generating a “training set” worth of samples from the previous strong learner, where samples are obtained via MCMC.

The experimental section is clearly the weak point of the paper. The method is evaluated on a synthetic dataset, and a single real-world dataset, MNIST: both for generation and as a feature extraction mechanism for classification. Of these, the synthetic experiments were the clearest in showcasing the method. On MNIST, the baseline models are much too weak for the results to be convincing. A modestly sized VAE can obtain 90 nats within hours on a single GPU, clearly an achievable goal. Furthermore, despite arguments to the contrary, I firmly believe that mixing base learners is an academic exercise, if only because of the burden of implementing K different models & training algorithms. This section fails to answer a more fundamental question: is it better to train a large VAE by maximizing the elbow, or e.g. train 10 iterations of boosting, using VAEs 1/10th the size of the baseline model ? Experimental details are also lacking, especially with respect to the sampling procedure used to draw samples from the BGM. The paper would also benefit from likelihood estimates obtained via AIS.

With regards to novelty and prior work, there is also a missing reference to “Self Supervised Boosting” by Welling et al [R1]. After a cursory read through, there seems to be strong similarities to the GenBGM approach which ought to be discussed.

Overall, I am on the fence. The idea of boosting generative models is intriguing, seems well motivated and has potential for impact. For this reason, and given the theoretical contributions, I am willing to overlook some of the issues highlighted above, and hope the authors can address some of them in time for the rebuttal.

[R1]

The authors propose to combine a CCA objective with a downstream loss.  This is a really nice and natural idea.  However, both the execution and presentation leave a lot to be desired in the current version of the paper.

It is not clear what the overall objective is.  This was asked in a pre-review question but the answer did not fully clarify it for me.  Is it the sum of the CCA objective and the final (top-layer) objective, including the CCA constraints?  Is there some interpolation of the two objectives?  

By saying that the top-layer objective is "cosine distance" or "squared cosine distance", do you really mean you are just minimizing this distance between the matched pairs in the two views?  If so, then of course that does not work out of the box without the intervening CCA layer:  You could minimize it by setting all of the projections to a single point.  A better comparison would be against a contrastive loss like the Hermann & Blunsom one mentioned in the reviewer question, which aims to both minimize the distance for matched pairs and separate mismatched ones (where "mismatched" ones can be uniformly drawn, or picked in some cleverer way).  But other discriminative top-layer objectives that are tailored to a downstream task could make sense.

There is some loose terminology in the paper.  The authors refer to the "correlation" and "cross-correlation" between two vectors.  "Correlation" normally applies to scalars, so you need to define what you mean here.  "Cross-correlation" typically refers to time series.  In eq. (2) you are taking the max of a matrix.  Finally I am not too sure in what way this approach is "fully differentiable" while regular CCA is not -- perhaps it is worth revisiting this term as well.

Also just a small note about the relationship between cosine distance and correlation:  they are related when we view the dimensions of each of the two vectors as samples of a single random variable.  In that case the cosine distance of the (mean-normalized) vectors is the same as the correlation between the two corresponding random variables.  In CCA we are viewing each dimension of the vectors as its own random variable.  So I fear the claim about cosine distance and correlation is a bit of a red herring here.

A couple of typos:

"prosed" --> "proposed"
"allong" --> "along"
The authors have withdrawn the submission.

This work presents an empirical study of the influence of different types of data augmentation on the performance of CNNs. It also proposes to incorporate additional loss functions to encourage approximate invariance or equivariance, and shows there are some benefits.

The paper reads well and the objectives are clear. The study of invariances in CNNs is a very important topic, and advances in this area are greatly appreciated. The paper splits itself in two very different parts -- the empirical study of equivariances in existing CNNs, and the proposal of equivariance objectives. However, taken separately each of these two parts could be better executed.

On the empirical study, its breath is relatively limited, and it's hard to draw any far-reaching conclusions from it:
- Only one network is studied; at least one other architecture would have made for better generalization.
- Only one layer (fc7) is studied; this presents issues as the top layer is the most invariant. At least one convolutional layer (possibly more) should have been considered.
- The reliance on the scanned text dataset does not help; however the ImageNet results are definitely very encouraging.

It is nice to see how performance degrades with the degree of transformations, and the authors do interpret the results, but it would be better to see more analysis. There is only a limited set of conclusions that can be drawn from evaluating networks with jittered data. If the authors could propose some other interesting ways to assess the invariance and equivariance, they would potentially draw more insightful conclusions from it.

On the proposed loss function, only a very quick treatment of it is given (Section 4, half a page). It does not differ too much from known invariance/equivariance objectives studied in the literature previously, e.g. Decoste and Scholkopf, "Training Invariant Support Vector Machines", Machine Learning, 2002.

I'm not sure that dividing the paper into these two different contributions is the best approach; they both feel a bit incomplete, and a full treatment of only one of them would make for an overall better paper.

I appreciate the authors putting a lot of effort into the rebuttal. But it seems that all the reviewers agree that the local trend features segmentation and computation is adhoc, and the support for accepting the paper is lukewarm.
 
 As an additional data point, I would argue that the model is not end-to-end since it doesn't address the aspect of segmentation. Incorporating that into the model would have made it much more interesting and novel.

This paper examines computational creativity from a machine learning perspective. Creativity is defined as a model's ability to generate new types of objects unseen during training. The authors argue that likelihood training and evaluation are by construction ill-suited for out-of-class generation and propose a new evaluation framework which relies on the use of held-out classes of objects to measure a model's ability to generate new and interesting object types.

I am not very familiar with the literature on computational creativity research, so I can't judge on how well this work has been put into the context of existing work. From a machine learning perspective, I find the ideas presented in this paper new, interesting and thought-provoking.

As I understand, the hypothesis is that the ability of a model to generate new and interesting types we *do not* know about correlates with its ability to generate new and interesting types we *do* know about, and the latter is a good proxy for the former. The extent to which this is true depends on the bias introduced by model selection. Just like when measuring generalization performance, one should be careful not to reuse the same held-out classes for model selection and for evaluation.

Nevertheless, I appreciate the effort that has been made to formalize the notion of computational creativity within the machine learning framework. I view it as an important first step in that direction, and I think it deserves its place at ICLR, especially given that the paper is well-written and approachable for machine learning researchers.

Hello,

I have several questions regarding the paper.

1. What is the attacker’s objective in your study? Is it to reconstruct the training data, or to determine if a particular sample was part of the training set?

2. If the aim is to reconstruct the training data, does the attacker need the exact data, or would an approximation suffice?

3. Regarding neural networks:
   - In your view, are there any attack techniques capable of recovering the exact training data from a trained model?
   - Do you believe there are defense strategies that could prevent an attacker from recovering even an approximate version of the training data?

4. How can we measure the robustness of a learning model—particularly a neural network—when no defensive measures are in place?

5. How can we assess the strength of a model that hasn’t been trained on the exact training data? For instance, certain adversarial training approaches avoid using clean data entirely; instead, the model is trained each epoch on varied adversarial examples generated from the original data.
   - How does the model retain information about the training data if 1) it never encounters the actual data, and 2) the training data differs across epochs?

6. How does your method’s performance compare to that of adversarial training?

This paper presents a heuristic method for training a deep directed generative model. In this model, each layer samples from the same conditional distribution, analogous to the transition operator in a Markov chain. The method approximates the gradient by substituting an alternative distribution for the posterior over latent variables, much like optimizing a variational lower bound. However, unlike variational optimization, the approximating distribution is not refined to improve the lower bound but is instead heuristically defined at each step. Another key difference is that the conditional distributions are optimized greedily, rather than by following the gradient of the joint log-likelihood.

The proposed approach is intriguing and appears worthy of further investigation. That said, given the existence of methods for training similar models that are theoretically more rigorous, comparably efficient, and proven effective in practice—such as the work by Rezende & Mohamed (2015)—I remain uncertain about its potential impact. A more significant concern is the limited scope of the empirical evaluation.

I commend the authors for providing proper log-likelihood estimates, which will facilitate future comparisons on continuous MNIST. However, they should clarify that the figures taken from Wu et al. (2016) do not reflect the typical performance of a variational autoencoder (VAE). As noted in that paper, the reported log-likelihood values should not be directly compared against models with more flexible observation models, which can achieve substantially higher scores.

It would have been valuable to include comparisons with inpainting results from other methods. How does the practicality of this approach measure up against alternatives? Similar to the diffusion method by Sohl-Dickstein et al. (2015), the proposed technique seems both efficient and effective for inpainting. Not emphasizing this point more and conducting thorough evaluations feels like a missed opportunity.

Minor points:
- Citations are needed for “ordered visible dimension sampling.”
- There are typos and repeated misuse of \citet

We have updated the manuscript with a revised version. To maintain consistency with an earlier study, we adjusted the subset of cells used to present results, though the findings themselves are unaffected. Because one of the example cells in Figure 3 no longer met the selection criteria, we now display responses from a different OFF example cell. Other updates include a supplementary figure comparing results with a normalized log-likelihood metric, a link to a video of the stimulus, the addition of "Responses" to the title, and minor visual refinements to the figures.

Thank you for conducting additional experiments and expanding the explanations in the manuscript. These revisions have addressed most of my concerns, and I have updated my evaluation score accordingly.

This work focuses on learning a generative function G that maps inputs from a source domain to a target domain, while ensuring that a given representation function f remains consistent across both domains—a criterion referred to as f-constancy. The proposed approach is evaluated on two visual domain adaptation tasks.

The paper is generally clear and well-structured, supported by extensive experimental results on both datasets. The concept of f-constancy represents the core contribution of the work.

However, it seems counterintuitive to constrain G to the form g ∘ f, since f is a restricted function that may already discard information. In the face dataset example, f is trained to optimize performance on a specific task using external data. It is unclear whether an input from the source or target domain can be accurately reconstructed via G as defined in equations (5) and (6). Additionally, f is learned with a particular objective in mind—for instance, digit recognition in the SVHN dataset or identity recognition in the face dataset. Consequently, the entire procedure would need to be repeated for domain adaptation between the same domains but with a different task, such as recognizing facial expressions rather than identity.

Could the authors provide insight into why the baseline method outlined in equations (1) and (2) performs so weakly?

Figure 5 offers a visual comparison between style transfer and the proposed method, though it is not evident which approach is superior. Would it be feasible to apply style transfer to generate emojis from photos and replicate the experiments presented in Table 4?

To achieve effective sample transfer from a source to a target domain, is it essential to also train the model to reconstruct images within the target domain? Are there any ablation studies that explore this?

An intriguing architectural approach accumulates and continuously refines errors as more of a video sequence is observed.

Clarity: The video you produced appears very useful for illustrating the information flow in your network—consider linking to it from the paper.

While our model, with hyperparameters tuned for KITTI, falls short of the performance achieved by Finn et al. (2016), it surpasses the prior state-of-the-art model from Mathieu et al. (2016).

Currently, it is unclear how distinct the training and test sequences are, as standard benchmarks for video prediction are not well established, and each researcher selects their preferred data. The underperformance relative to Finn et al. (2016) on the H3.6m Walking videos is somewhat disappointing.

This paper presents a strong application of A3C to the game Doom, augmented with a set of techniques that enabled it to win a VizDoom competition. While the competitive focus could easily have come at the expense of scientific rigor—such as systematically justifying each design choice—the authors manage to provide reasonable justification for their approach.

However, two of my concerns (outlined by AnonReviewer2 below) were not addressed.

Furthermore, the references provided are somewhat limited. For instance, reward shaping and progressively challenging task configurations both have extensive bodies of literature, with roots reaching back to Mark Ring’s work in the 1990s. There is also substantial related research on other first-person shooter games. I am not suggesting direct comparisons with prior work, but the paper would benefit from offering readers a clearer sense of the broader research context.

This paper introduces a model that learns to generate programs from input-output examples, using a domain-specific language for string transformations comparable to Excel’s Flash Fill. The method builds a program tree incrementally, guided by embeddings of the provided examples. Extension probabilities are derived from embeddings of leaves and production rules—a key innovation is the “Recursive-Reverse-Recursive Neural Network” (R3NN), which computes context-aware leaf embeddings through a differentiable, belief-propagation-like process over the tree.

The paper has several strengths. Unlike some deep learning research, this work feels applicable in real-world settings in the near term. The R3NN is well motivated and clearly explained. The authors thoroughly investigate model variations to identify effective components. The writing is also clear and engaging, making the lengthy paper enjoyable to read.

Some limitations remain. Accuracy is still not high, possibly because the model is trained on short programs but tested on longer ones—it is unclear why training wasn’t extended to longer programs. The model also uses a fixed number of input-output examples; additional examples cannot be incorporated and may even reduce performance, as shown in the experiments. Nevertheless, the paper is suitable for acceptance at ICLR.

Additional notes:
- The expansion probability expression contains many exponentials (“e” terms); using “Softmax” might simplify it.
- Mention is made of a bidirectional LSTM for processing leaf representations before scoring, but details are lacking.
- The authors emphasize the importance of hyperbolic tangent activations; further explanation of why alternatives like ReLU are less suitable would be valuable.
- Batching is unclear given varying tree structures across programs. More details on optimization (e.g., SGD, Adam), learning rates, and weight initialization would improve reproducibility.
- For Figure 6 (unsolved benchmarks), including program sizes would help clarify whether failures are due to program length or other factors.

This paper introduces a model that learns to generate programs from input-output examples, using a domain-specific language capable of handling a broad range of string transformations, much like Excel’s Flash Fill. The method builds a program tree step by step, guided by embeddings derived from the example pairs. Extension probabilities are determined from embeddings of leaf nodes and production rules. A key contribution is the "Recursive-Reverse-Recursive Neural Network" (R3NN), which computes globally informed leaf embeddings through a process akin to belief propagation on trees, trained end-to-end with differentiability.

The paper has several strengths. Unlike some deep learning research, this work feels applicable in real-world settings soon. The R3NN concept is well motivated and thoughtfully explained. The authors also test numerous model variations to identify effective components. Additionally, the writing is clear and engaging, making the lengthy paper enjoyable to read.

Some limitations are noted. Accuracy remains modest, possibly because the model trains on short programs but must infer longer ones—why not train on longer programs directly? The model also uses a fixed number of input-output examples; extra examples cannot be incorporated and may even reduce performance, as shown in experiments. Nevertheless, the paper merits acceptance at ICLR.

Minor points:
- The expansion probability formula contains many exponentials; "Softmax" would be clearer.
- Mention is made of a bidirectional LSTM for processing leaf representations before scoring, but specifics are lacking.
- The authors emphasize hyperbolic tangent activations; more discussion on why alternatives like ReLU are unsuitable would be helpful.
- Batching is unclear given varying tree structures per program. Details on optimization (e.g., SGD, Adam), learning rates, and weight initialization would improve reproducibility.
- For unsolved benchmarks in Figure 6, including program sizes would clarify if failures relate to program length or other factors.
- Relevant work by Piech et al. on learning program embeddings

The reviewers find the approach compelling and the findings valuable. While they have raised several questions and suggestions for improvement, these appear manageable to resolve, and I am confident the paper will be significantly strengthened through revision.

This paper introduces an algorithm designed to enhance exploration in policy search reinforcement learning. The approach optimizes a weighted sum of expected reward—similar to the REINFORCE objective without entropy regularization—and a component drawn from the reward-augmented maximum likelihood objective (based on a recent NIPS paper). The authors demonstrate that this update can be implemented with only minor adjustments to the standard REINFORCE algorithm. Improved results are shown on several sequential, program-like tasks such as string copying and addition.

I recommend accepting this paper, as the contribution is solid and the method provides a straightforward yet effective modification to REINFORCE that yields better exploration than typical Boltzmann policies. However, upon reflection, my enthusiasm has tempered somewhat, as the advance over prior work—specifically the RAML paper—does not appear especially significant. The experimental domains do not seem particularly demanding, and outperforming REINFORCE alone is not necessarily remarkable.

Strengths:
+ A well-motivated, simple adjustment to REINFORCE that improves exploration
+ Clear performance gains with relatively little hyperparameter tuning

Weaknesses:
− The improvement over the RAML approach is unclear; the method essentially combines REINFORCE and RAML (though within an RL framework)
− Experiments are adequate but not exceptional compared to simpler baselines

This paper presents a model that combines concepts from generative topic modeling with recurrent neural network language modeling. The proposed method is evaluated on both document classification and language modeling benchmarks, where it performs well. The authors also analyze the topics learned by the model and its text generation capabilities. Overall, the paper is clearly written, and with the promised code, others should be able to reproduce the approach. I have two significant questions for the authors to address:

1. LDA topic models rely on an exchangeability (bag-of-words) assumption. The generative description of TopicRNN should explicitly state whether it makes the same assumption. On the surface, it appears to, since y_t is sampled using only the document topic vector and h_t. However, in practice, h_t is derived from a recurrent model that observes y_{t-1}. It is unclear how this clean generative model relates to the actual implementation. In the section on generating sequential text, it is evident that the topic model cannot generate words without using y_{1:t-1}, which seems inconsistent with the generative model specification. This should be clarified in the paper to ensure completeness.

2. The topic model permits only linear interactions with the topic vector theta. This may be necessary to maintain tractability in the generative model, but it appears to be a limiting assumption. Ideally, the topic representation would interact nonlinearly with the language model to adjust word probabilities in a document in richer ways. Please discuss the reasoning behind this modeling choice and, if possible, suggest how future work might relax this assumption—or explain why it is not as restrictive as it may seem.

Additionally, the colors in Figure 2 are very difficult to distinguish.

This paper introduces a sophisticated algebraic framework for analyzing the types of functions representable by convolutional networks. Like many similar efforts in the literature, the idealized networks it describes—which must be interpretable as polynomials over tensors—differ from practical CNNs; for example, the ReLU nonlinearity is replaced by a product of linear functions (or a sum of logs). Although the paper is highly technical, each concept is clearly defined and mathematical terms are properly introduced. Still, the authors could make the core ideas more accessible, offering a clearer intuitive grasp of what separation rank means before layering on mathematical interpretations. My own algebra, dating back to the SVM era, is rusty, and I am unfamiliar with the separation rank framework—it would have been easier to first fully understand a simple, gentle case (like the shallow network in Section 5.3) before tackling the general deep case.

To summarize my understanding of the key result in Theorem 1:

- In the shallow case, the upper bound on separation rank shows that this rank grows at most linearly with network size (as measured by the single hidden layer). Exponential network sizes arise only when the rank must grow exponentially to satisfy a given partition.
- In the deep case, the upper bound is also linear in network size (measured by the last hidden layer), but this linearity stems from a specific partition choice (I^low, J^high). The maximal rank induced by this partition is inherently linear, so the network size can remain linear.

My attempt to capture the main point likely falls short, which underscores the complexity of partition rank. Its linear growth with network size can be beneficial or detrimental depending on the context. Hopefully, a concise, single-slide explanation will emerge someday.

While the paper in its current form is suitable for conference publication, I have two suggestions that could enhance its significance:

On the theoretical side, we remain far from the completeness

This paper introduces a new type of recurrent network designed to learn hierarchical structures in sequential data—for example, progressing from characters to words. Unlike previous approaches, the method does not rely on explicit boundary markers to segment sequences into meaningful units (such as in Chung et al., 2016).

The model is structured across multiple layers, each capturing information at a different level of abstraction. Lower layers activate higher ones and determine when to update them via a controller, or state cell (denoted as c). A notable aspect of the design is that c is discrete, which may enable faster inference. However, this discrete nature also complicates training, leading the authors to employ the straight-through estimator (Hinton, 2012).

Experiments are comprehensive, and the model achieves competitive results across several challenging tasks. Qualitative analysis further indicates that it can identify natural boundaries in data.

Overall, the paper presents a robust and innovative model supported by promising experimental outcomes.

On a minor note, there are a few observations regarding the writing and related work:

- In the introduction:  
  The claim, “One of the key principles of learning in deep neural networks as well as in the human brain,” should be supported by evidence for the “human brain” portion.  
  The statement, “For modelling temporal data, the recent resurgence of recurrent neural networks (RNN) has led to remarkable advances,” appears to omit Mikolov et al. (2010) from the references.  
  Regarding the phrase, “in spite of the fact that hierarchical multiscale structures naturally exist in many temporal data,” a reference to Lin et al. (1996) is missing.

- In the related work:  
  It is noted that “A more recent model, the clockwork RNN (CW-RNN) (Koutník et al., 2014) extends the hierarchicalRNN (El Hihi & Beng

The authors introduce an input-switched affine network for character-level language modeling—a recurrent architecture that omits pointwise nonlinearities and instead switches its transition matrix and bias based on the input character. This design is driven by interpretability, as it enables the decomposition of output contributions into terms like κ_s^t and allows the use of linear algebra to examine the network’s behavior.

As a reviewer, I believe I have grasped the paper’s core ideas and arguments, though I am not an expert in RNN-based language models or interpretability in machine learning. I am not aware of closely related prior work; the most similar research I know of involves deconvolutional networks for interpreting vision CNNs.

Strengths:
This is original, high-quality work that is clearly written and reflects considerable effort. I found Section 4.5, which contrasts projection into a readout subspace with a “computational” subspace, particularly interesting and substantive.

Weaknesses:
My primary reservations concern the persuasiveness of both the proposed model and its analysis:
1. The ISAN model is only evaluated on a small-scale task (text8), leaving its potential as a strong character-level language model for larger tasks uncertain.
2. The analysis sections do not yield substantial insight into the learned network.

Additional concerns:
1b. The ISAN architecture appears suited mainly to small-vocabulary (character-based) language modeling, and may not extend to general RNNs with large discrete vocabularies or continuous inputs.
2a. While the analysis includes appealing visualizations and inventive metrics, it offers limited concrete understanding.
2b. It is unclear which analytical aspects are specific to ISAN and which might generalize to nonlinear RNNs.
2c. Regarding Sections 4.2–4.3, the central quantity κ_s^t does not appear especially meaningful. For instance, in Figure 2, the

This paper presents a time-aware recommender system built on point processes, which are parameterized by dynamic latent representations of users and items. These representations are modeled as coupled autoregressive processes—specifically, each user’s or item’s representation updates when an interaction occurs, depending on both representations before time *t*. This interaction-driven coevolution is implemented using a recurrent neural network. The model can also integrate heterogeneous inputs. Experiments on multiple datasets compare the approach against various baselines.

Key contributions include: (1) formulating recommendations through parameterized point processes with dynamics governed by latent user/item representations, (2) an optimization algorithm to maximize the likelihood of this process, employing techniques that reduce its inherent complexity, and (3) experimental evaluation for time-sensitive recommendation.

A prior paper by the same authors (NIPS 2016) describes a closely related model of continuous-time coevolution and uses a similar evaluation. The differences are subtle—the point process model and latent factor dynamics vary slightly—but the overall modeling approach and rationale remain essentially identical. It is unclear what specifically leads to improved performance over the NIPS model: is it the choice of point process or the new parameterization? Both are quite similar. The authors do not justify their choice of point process form in either paper, nor do they indicate whether alternative forms were tested. Similarly, the dynamical process uses a sigmoid nonlinearity for the latent vectors, which may not differ significantly from a linear model, yet its role is not examined. Some inconsistencies also appear between the results in the two papers.

Regarding evaluation, two criteria are introduced. The method for evaluating item recommendation is not entirely clear: it is stated that at each time *t*, the model predicts the item a user will interact with. Does this refer to the next item after time *t*? For time prediction, its relevance as a recommendation metric is not explained. A comparison of complexity

This paper presents a method for approximating the FastText model to reduce its memory footprint by several orders of magnitude while maintaining classification accuracy. The original FastText approach uses a linear classifier over bag-of-words embeddings, which allows for fast training and evaluation but results in a large model size.

The work applies lossy compression techniques to approximate the original model. Specifically, the embedding and classifier matrices A and B are compressed using Product Quantization, and an aggressive dictionary pruning strategy is applied. Experiments across datasets with varying numbers of classes are used to tune parameters and validate the method. With only a minor drop in accuracy, the model size is reduced dramatically—by approximately 100 to 1000 times compared to the original.

The paper is clearly written, with a well-defined goal and thorough experimentation. It evaluates and compares different compression options, such as Product Quantization versus Locality-Sensitive Hashing, which adds useful insight. However, the paper does not introduce fundamentally new ideas for text classification; instead, it adapts existing lossy compression techniques. Contributions include:
- A simple adaptation of Product Quantization for unnormalized vectors,
- Formulating dictionary pruning as a set covering problem (NP-hard) and showing that a greedy approach works well in practice,
- Direct use of hashing tricks and Bloom filters from prior work.

These techniques are general and could be applied in other contexts.

Some minor issues are noted:
- The calculation of total model size is unclear. What components are included, and what proportion of the size comes from matrices A and B, the dictionary, and other elements? Identifying the size bottleneck—which may vary with the number of classes—is difficult. A formula for total model size in terms of parameters (such as k and b for PQ, K for the dictionary, and number of classes) would have been helpful.
- Certain sections lack clarity. For example, the greedy dictionary pruning method

The authors introduced leaky rectified linear units as a replacement for binary units in Gaussian Restricted Boltzmann Machines (RBMs). They also presented a sampling method for training these leaky-ReLU RBMs. In experiments, likelihood estimates on Cifar10 and SVHN datasets were provided using Annealed Importance Sampling (AIS).

Exploring alternative nonlinear hidden units in RBMs is a promising direction. However, several aspects of the current work raise questions:

1. The correctness of the proposed sampling method (Algorithm 2) is not justified, and the computational overhead from the inner loop and projection steps should be addressed.
2. The reported performance of the Gaussian RBM—both in likelihood values and generated samples—appears significantly lower than expected, suggesting possible issues in training.
3. Effective generative models often improve classification when labeled data is scarce, and Gaussian RBMs have shown strength in texture synthesis where mixing is critical. Further experiments in these two areas would strengthen the work.

This paper introduces a modified version of the Restricted Boltzmann Machine (RBM) that replaces the standard sigmoid nonlinearity with a leaky ReLU activation. By gradually annealing the leakiness coefficient—effectively transitioning from a Gaussian to a non-Gaussian model—the authors achieve a higher mixing rate during sampling. Using the same annealing approach, they also improve the accuracy of partition function estimation for the new model.

Main comments:

The proposed model is limited to real-valued data. However, RBMs are mainly applied to binary data, and Gaussian RBMs for real-valued data are not widely established. To better demonstrate the model's advantages, the authors should include comparisons using binary data. Additionally, evaluating the model on only two datasets is insufficient for a newly proposed method.

The statement that the marginal distribution of visible variables follows a truncated Gaussian is inaccurate. In a truncated normal distribution, variable values are restricted to a specific range, such as requiring variable v to lie within an interval [a1, a2].

This paper introduces a new model-based reinforcement learning method and tests it on three ATARI games. The method trains a model to predict sequences of rewards and life-loss probabilities based on a history of frames and a sequence of actions. A controller samples random action sequences and selects the one that best balances the likelihood of gaining a point against the risk of losing a life, according to predefined thresholds. The system achieves super-human performance on each game individually, as well as when trained jointly on all three in a multi-task setting.

While the results are promising, the system relies on several ad-hoc design decisions, and the paper offers limited analysis of the contribution of its individual components.

Primary concerns:
- Combining predicted rewards and life-loss probabilities is handled in an ad-hoc manner. A more natural approach would involve learning a Q-value; instead, different rules are crafted for different games.
- It is unclear whether the model is genuinely being learned and improved. Presenting predictions for multiple action sequences from selected starting states—both in successful and unsuccessful games—would help. Tracking learning progress by plotting training loss on a fixed holdout set of sequences would also be valuable.
- How critical is the proposed RRNN architecture? Would performance persist without residual connections? Could a standard LSTM achieve similar results?

Minor points:
- The second paragraph of the introduction overlooks earlier work on model-based RL, such as Dyna and Lin and Mitchell’s “Memory approaches to reinforcement learning in non-Markovian domains.”
- In Section 3.1, using a_i to denote observations is unconventional; o_i for observations and a_i for actions would be clearer.
- In Section 3.2.2, the notation r_i is reused after previously representing the reward at time i.
- Observation 1 seems somewhat tangential; citing the layer normalization paper for motivation would suffice.
- In the second-to-last paragraph of Section 3

This paper reinterprets paragraph vectors from a generative perspective, which provides a rationale for the existing inference method and for applying L2 regularization to paragraph embeddings. It also supports jointly training a classifier on these vectors for text classification.

There are several citation inconsistencies throughout the text and bibliography, such as inconsistent use of authors’ first names. Using a tool like BibTex would help standardize the references. The work appears to offer limited novelty.

The authors incorrectly state that no method exists for inferring vectors for unseen documents in paragraph vector models. The original paragraph vector paper describes fixing other model parameters and using gradient descent to infer a new paragraph vector, which does not require the original dataset. The present approach, which finds a MAP estimate for new vectors, seems functionally equivalent. Therefore, the main contribution of the generative framing appears to be the regularization applied to the embedding matrix.

The supervised version essentially involves jointly training a linear classifier on paragraph vectors, while inference remains the same. For the n-gram based method, a citation to Li et al. (2015) is needed.

In the experiments, Tables 1 and 2 are poorly formatted, with truncated decimal places (e.g., “.0” missing). The paragraph vector size is not specified. Additionally, the reported SGPV results are lower than those in the original paragraph vector paper, which achieved 48.7 on SST-1 and 86.3 on SST-2.

Reference:  
Li, B., Liu, T., Du, X., Zhang, D., & Zhao, Z. (2015). Learning Document Embeddings by Predicting N-grams for Sentiment Classification of Long Movie Reviews.

UPDATE: I have reviewed the responses in this thread. My position remains unchanged.

The authors present deep VCCA, a deep probabilistic CCA model that employs neural networks to parameterize likelihoods. They apply variational inference via an inference network and reparameterization gradients. An extension called VCCA-private is also proposed, incorporating local latent variables for each data view. A link to multi-view autoencoders is demonstrated as well.

Given the rise of black-box variational inference and variational autoencoders, the methodological contributions in papers like this one are arguably limited. The model essentially extends probabilistic CCA by using neural networks in the likelihoods. The inference procedure follows standard black-box techniques, relying on reparameterization gradients and inference networks. The use of a mean-field approximation also feels dated, especially in light of recent advances in more flexible approximations (e.g., Rezende & Mohamed, 2015; Tran et al., 2016).

While the connection to multi-view autoencoders initially seems valuable, it ultimately boils down to the familiar distinction between MAP estimation and variational inference. The authors highlight sampling as a key difference, but the KL regularizer is what truly matters. Even with noisy samples, a normal variational approximation would see its variances shrink to zero, effectively reducing to a point mass—equivalent to optimizing the MVAE objective. (The authors seem partially aware of this, based on comments in the paper, though their stance is ambiguous.)

Nevertheless, the paper excels in its applied contributions. The experiments are robust, comparing the approach to other multi-view methods across several interesting datasets. Although the "private variables" are conceptually simple, they effectively separate view-specific latent representations from shared ones. It would have been beneficial to include comparisons with probabilistic inference methods, such as fully Bayesian linear CCA.

The paper also adopts several approximations that have become commonplace but may not be essential—like mean-field families and inference networks

This paper introduces an unsupervised learning approach for convolutional neural networks, using distances between patches from the same image and from different images. The method offers little novelty, as it closely resembles earlier work by Doersch et al. (2015) and Dosovitsky et al. (2015). Furthermore, evaluation is confined to small datasets, reducing the potential significance of the contribution.

The self-supervised loss employs a Siamese architecture, aiming to bring patches from the same image closer together in feature space compared to a contrasting patch from a different, randomly selected image. This approach shares a similar motivation with the methods introduced by Doersch et al. (ICCV 2015) and Isola et al. (ICLR 2016 workshop). In fact, the proposed loss appears to be a streamlined variant of Doersch et al.’s work, as it omits the spatial offset—a readily available self-supervised cue in natural images. Intuitively, the self-supervised task defined here seems less challenging and thus potentially less powerful than the earlier approach. A more detailed comparison between these two methods would be valuable. Still, the proposed technique proves effective in delivering strong empirical results despite its simplicity. Additional implementation specifics would enhance clarity, such as the impact of patch size, the degree of overlap between sampled patches, and any key strategies used to prevent trivial solutions.
